coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent metal-doped carbon dots for neuronal manipulations
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Vijay Bhooshan
Kumar, Raj
Gedanken, Aharon
Shefi, Orit
coredata.dc:description: There is a growing need for biocompatible nanocomposites that may efficiently interact with biological tissues through multiple modalities. Carbon dots (CDs) could serve as biocompatible fluorescence nanomaterials for targeted tissue/cell imaging. Important goals toward this end are to enhance the fluorescence quantum yields of the CDs and to increase their targetability to cells. Here, sonochemistry was used to develop a one-pot synthesis of CDs, including metal-doped CDs (M@CDs), demonstrating how various experimental parameters, such as sonication time, temperature, and power of sonication affect the size of the CDs (2–10 nm) and their fluorescence properties. The highest measured quantum yield of emission was ∼16%. Similarly, we synthesized CDs doped with different metals (M@CDs) including Ga, Sn, Zn, Ag, and Au. The interaction of M@CDs with neuron-like cells was examined and showed efficient uptake and low cytotoxicity. Moreover, the influence of the M@CDs on the improvement of neurites during initiation and elongation growth phases were compared with pristine CDs. Our research demonstrates the use of M@CDs for imaging and for neuronal interactions. The M@CD nanocomposites are promising due to their biocompatibility, photo-stability and potential selective affinity, paving the way for multifunctional biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-doped carbon dots
Sonication
Fluorescence
Neural tissue engineering
Biocompatibility
Neurite outgrowth

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Flow cytometry analyses of cellular uptake
Physical and chemical characterization of metal-doped CDs
Interactions of metal-doped carbon dots with living cells

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectroscopy of (a) CDs and M@CDs, (b) Ga@CDs, (c) Zn@CDs, (d) Sn@CDs, (e) Ag@CDs, and (f) Au@CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
UV/Vis spectroscopy of the CDs and M@CDs: (a) at a small scale, and (b) magnified, focusing on the shorter wavelengths, up to 600 nm.
Zeta potential of (a) CDs and M@CDs, (b) Ga@CDs, (c) Zn@CDs, (d) Sn@CDs, (e) Ag@CDs, and (f) Au@CDs.
Transmission electron microscopy of CDs (a,b), and M@CDs; Ga@CDs (c,d), Sn@CDs (e,f), Ag@CDs (g,h), Zn@CDs (i,j) and Au@CDs (k,l).

Cytotoxicity of metal doped carbon dots in PC12 cells at different concentrations and time points (black represents 24 h and red represents 72 h) obtained by the XTT cell viability assay, using cells without nanoparticles as control. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Light microscopy images of PC12 cells differentiation at different time points in the absence and presence of CDs and M@CDs. Scale bar = 100 μm.
Average of fluorescence intensity (mean) upon incubation of the cells in the absence and presence of various prepared samples.
Confocal microscope images of SH-SY5Y cells after 24 h incubation with the prepared nanoparticles. Left: bright field imaging, middle: fluorescent imaging and right: overlap image. Scale bar = 10 μm.
The synthesis process and possible formation mechanism of M@CDs nanoparticles.
Summary of CDs and M@CDs properties. QY – quantum yield.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent metal-doped carbon dots for neuronal manipulations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA) and Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat Gan 5290002, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA) and Department of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA) and Faculty of Engineering, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat Gan 5290002, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA) and Faculty of Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is a growing need for biocompatible nanocomposites that may efficiently interact with biological tissues through multiple modalities. Carbon dots (CDs) could serve as biocompatible fluorescence nanomaterials for targeted tissue/cell imaging. Important goals toward this end are to enhance the fluorescence quantum yields of the CDs and to increase their targetability to cells. Here, sonochemistry was used to develop a one-pot synthesis of CDs, including metal-doped CDs (M@CDs), demonstrating how various experimental parameters, such as sonication time, temperature, and power of sonication affect the size of the CDs (2–10 nm) and their fluorescence properties. The highest measured quantum yield of emission was ∼16%. Similarly, we synthesized CDs doped with different metals (M@CDs) including Ga, Sn, Zn, Ag, and Au. The interaction of M@CDs with neuron-like cells was examined and showed efficient uptake and low cytotoxicity. Moreover, the influence of the M@CDs on the improvement of neurites during initiation and elongation growth phases were compared with pristine CDs. Our research demonstrates the use of M@CDs for imaging and for neuronal interactions. The M@CD nanocomposites are promising due to their biocompatibility, photo-stability and potential selective affinity, paving the way for multifunctional biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials with photoluminescence (PL) such as semiconductor quantum dots, upconversion nanoparticles, graphene nanocomposites and luminescent metal complexes with novel physicochemical properties, have shown great impact in biomedical applications [1–5]. Since their finding in 2006, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention due to their unique photoluminescence properties and increasing importance in scientific and technological applications [6]. Carbon dots are widely employed in bio/chemo sensors [7], bioimaging [8,9], cell imaging [10,11], dye degradation [12], chemiluminescence [13], solar cells [14], photocatalysts [15], nanoelectronic devices [16], photodynamic therapy [17], light emitting diodes [18] and gene delivery [17]. However, synthesis of CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is still a challenge. A large number of techniques were attempted for the preparation of CDs, including laser ablation, pyrolysis, combustion, hydrothermal processes and sonochemistry [19]. Sonochemistry is gaining more interest in recent years due to green approach and absence of direct interaction of molecular species with ultrasound due to acoustic wavelengths are much larger compare to molecules [20]. It is widely employing for synthesis of nanoparticles of various materials including carbon dots (CDs) [21]. In order to enhance the fluorescence and to functionalize the CDs further, doping by either metals or other atoms or molecules has been suggested. Various researchers tried doping the CDs by various materials to improve their fluorescence quantum yield [22]. Most of the studies reported so far used nitrogen doping (N@CDs) that leads to enhanced fluorescence [23–26]. Recently the advantages of metal and metal ion doping have been demonstrated. Europium (Eu)-doped CDs were synthesized by a rapid and simple microwave mediated method using polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a precursor [27]. The Eu@CDs showed a uniform and small particle size and exhibit good photostability and high PL quantum yields. The gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) were prepared by microwave-assisted polyol method and shows green fluorescence [28]. The Gd-CDs revealed a low cytotoxicity and showed optically label the C6 glioma cells. Cu-N doped CDs were also synthesized through a one-step pyrolytic synthesis with Na2[Cu(EDTA)] as precursor. The Cu species chelates with the carbon matrix through Cu-N complexes [29]. The photocatalytic efficiency of CDs in a photo-oxidation reaction was improved by doping of Sn with TiO2 [15]. Here we used sonication process for preparation of carbon dots due to feasibility to avoid use of hazardous chemicals and incorporation of impurities which leads to high toxicity of final product to formulated metal (Ga, Sn, Zn, Ag and Au) doped carbon dots (M@CDs) with low toxicity for neuroengineering applications.

Recently, we developed a facile sonochemical method for the synthesis of M@CDs, where the M are low melting point metals, such as Ga, In, Sn and Bi. In the process of investigating the properties of the M@CDs, we found that Ga@CDs showed antibacterial and promoting neuronal growth properties [30,31]. The synthesis of the M@CDs was carried out via sonochemistry of molten metals overlayered by silicon oil/organic liquids, and for the four metals (Ga, In, Sn and Bi) we found that the supernatant contains metal-doped carbon dots. The synthesis of Ga@CDs was described in detail [30,31]. The synthesis process yielded nanoparticles with an average diameter of 5 ± 3 nm. Later, we discovered that the Ga@CDs have biocidal properties, efficiently killing both sensitive and resistant strains of Pseudomonas aeruginosa [30]. It was revealed that the physical properties of Ga@CDs are different from those of pristine CDs.

Such particles may be also beneficial for tissue engineering. One of the most important therapeutic challenges is the recovery of the nervous system following an injury. Transection of a nerve by severe injury results in loss of function, nerve degeneration, scar tissue formation, and loss of communication between neurons and cells [32]. Major efforts are devoted to the development of therapeutic platforms, yet, the healing process is limited and there is an unmet need for platforms for detection and imaging and for drug delivery. In the last decade, various innovative and promising approaches were developed through various engineering technologies and nanomaterials [33]. Moreover, application of nanomaterials in neuroengineering is limited due to cytotoxicity leading to side effects in the body and due to difficulties in crossing the Blood Brain Barrier (BBB) [34]. However, in the last few years, various nanomaterials were found to show promising results, especially in neural tissue engineering – an alternative technology to prevent or treat neural injuries [35–37]. Various types of nanomaterial-based engineering approaches have been developed and are under investigation [38,39]. It was shown that interactions of neurons with nanometric elements promote regeneration of neurons, along with neuronal differentiation processes, improving neurite outgrowth and aligned formation of axons [40]. We thoroughly investigated the effects of different nanomaterials in neural tissue engineering. For example, we used metal nanostructures, namely gold [41], silver [42–45], silica nanostructures [46], magnetic nanoparticles [47,48], cues [49], collagen hydrogel [50,51] and recently also CDs [52,53]. Conjoining CDs with metal doping is of great advancement in the field of tissue engineering and reformative medicine and may lead to additive effects.

In this work we report a one-step sonochemical synthesis of CDs doped with five different metals (Ag, Au, Ga, Sn and Zn) that exhibit stable long-lasting fluorescence. According to our knowledge, the synthesis of Ag@CDs and Au@CDs is reported for the first time, as the synthetic route of the low melting point metals (Ga, In, Bi, Sn, Pb and Zn) that does not apply for the doping of metals with high melting point, such as Ag and Au. The physical and chemical characteristics of M@CDs were studied by several analytical tools, including fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), inductively coupled plasma – optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) and zeta potential measurements. We examined the impact of the different metal-doped carbon dots, on neural cells, on differentiation and growth. These prepared M@CDs exhibits low toxicity, biocompatibility, and good level of fluorescence that may therefore be of benefit for neural tissue engineering and biomedical applications.

Polyethylene glycol-400 (99.998%), metallic gallium, metallic tin, metallic zinc, gold chloride and silver nitrate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiments. RPMI cell culture medium and components, presumably, the components are horse serum (HS), fetal bovine serum (FBS), L-glutamine, penicillin-streptomycin and amphotericin were purchased from Biological Industries, Israel. Murine β-nerve growth factor (β-NGF) was purchased from Peprotech Ltd., Israel. All other chemicals and solvents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Israel.

CDs and M@CDs were synthesized by a modified sonochemical method as described in our previously published paper [19]. 12 mL of polyethylene glycol (PEG-400) and 50 mg of corresponding metal (Ag, Ag, Ga, Sn and Zn) were taken in a test tube and the tip of an ultrasonic transducer was dipped in the PEG solution, ∼1 in. above the bottom of the test tube. The ultrasonic transducer (model VCX 750) was obtained from Sonics and Materials Inc., USA (frequency 20 kHz, 230 V AC). The ultrasonic irradiation was applied in five different sets of experiments for the five different metals at 70% amplitude for 3 h. In parallel, M@CDs were deposited on a polyethylene surface by sonication.

The fluorescence properties of CDs and M@CDs were measured by a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging of M@CDs were analyzed by JEOL 2100, with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The analyzed samples for TEM measurements were prepared with isopropanol by evaporating a drop of PEG containing CDs on a carbon coated copper TEM grid. Elemental analysis and mapping were performed using high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Optical microscopy and laser scanning confocal microscopy (Carl Zeiss, Germany, model 710 NLO) were used to study the fluorescence properties of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses was recorded by an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a monochromatic X-ray source with Al Ka excitation (1486.6 eV). Binding energy calibration was based on C 1s at 285 eV. Zeta potential measurements of M@CDs were accomplished on a ZetaSizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK).

Rat pheochromocytoma PC12 cells (ATCC), containing 5% CO2, were cultured in a humidified incubator at 37 °C and grown regularly in 75 cm2 plastic tissue-culture flasks with 10 mL of RPMI medium, with 1% L-glutamine, 1% penicillin–streptomycin, 0.2% amphotericin and 10% FBS. Fresh 10 mL of medium were added every two days until reaching 40 mL. After sufficient growth, the cells were centrifuged, washed with fresh medium, and suspended into new medium with a syringe for further growth. PC12 cells were used for viability, differentiation and FACS analysis.

Human neuroblastoma, SH-SY5Y cells, containing 5% CO2, were cultured in a humidified incubator at 37 °C and grown regularly in 75 cm2 plastic tissue-culture flasks with 10 mL of Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium, with 1% L-glutamine, 1% penicillin–streptomycin, 0.2% amphotericin and 10% FBS. The medium was replaced every two days. Upon reaching a confluence of ∼80%, the cells were split by adding 5 mL trypsin. SH-SY5Y cells were used for confocal imaging.

The cytotoxicity of the CDs and M@CDs was investigated using the XTT assay, based on the ability of metabolic active cells to reduce the tetrazolium salt XTT to orange-colored derivatives of formazan. The number of metabolic active cells is proportional to the absorbance intensity of the dye. In 96-well plates, PC12 cells were seeded with different concentrations of CDs and M@CDs, and unseeded cells were used as control. The concentrations of the CDs and M@CDs were varied in the following range: 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 mg/mL and cultured for 24 and 72 h. XTT reaction solution (Biological Industries, Israel) was then added to the wells, and the plates were incubated for 5 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Absorbance was measured at 450 and 630 nm (background) using a spectrophotometer (BioTek Synergy 4, Vermont, USA).

Collagen type 1 was used to coat the plates for PC12 cells differentiation. A mixture of 1.5 mL of 0.01 M HCl and 24 µL of collagen (3.79 mg/mL) were added to each well of a 6-well plate followed by sterilization by UV treatment for 1 h. After sterilization, the plates were washed with 1× PBS three times, repeatedly. Collagen-coated plates were then seeded with PC12 cells in serum-reduced media (1% HS) for 24 h. After incubation of the cells for 24 h, murine β-NGF (50 ng/mL) was added to the medium. Cells were rinsed with PBS and fresh medium, and β-NGF was added every two days. Imaging was performed at different time intervals using a light microscope (Leica DMIL LED) at different areas of each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analyses of cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: PC12 cells were incubated with prepared CDs and M@CDs at concentrations of 10, 50 and 100 mg/mL. After 24 h, the cells were centrifuged and suspended with a syringe in fresh medium, washed with fresh medium thrice, and collected in the dark. The fluorescence intensity was measured by flow cytometry (FACS Aria III, BD Bioscience, USA) with laser excitation at 405 nm and emission-filtered at 450 nm, with bandwidth 40 nm. Cells without nanoparticles were used as control.

SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in cover slip containing 35 mm dish with nanoparticles at a concentration of 0.02 mg/mL. After 24 h, the medium was completely removed and treated with 1 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min, followed by washing with 1× PBS three times at 5-minute intervals at room temperature. Cells containing cover slip were then mounted on glass slides using an aqueous solution of immune mounting medium. Confocal imaging was done using Leica STED confocal fluorescent microscopy.

The synthesized metal-doped CDs were yellow to brownish-red in color; they were analyzed by various physico-chemical techniques and subsequently exploited for long-lasting stability examination, as well as for assessment of their biocompatibility. The M@CDs were used as a probe for fluorescence imaging of neuron-like cells (PC12 and SHSY-5Y), and their potential for assisting neuronal differentiation was examined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical characterization of metal-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The water soluble M@CDs synthesized from PEG-400 solution by the sonochemical technique contain many different functional groups, such as –COOH, –OH, –O– and –COOR, offering different chemical reactions, as discussed in the experimental section and shown schematically in Scheme 1 . The fluorescence properties of pristine CDs and M@CDs were explored.

CDs and M@CDs were prepared using PEG-400 and corresponding molten metal only Ga melts at 30 °C. The visual information of the aqueous suspension of CDs and M@CDs nanoparticles was obtained with the help of a camera in the presence of normal light. The CDs were pale-yellow in colour, while the M@CDs nanoparticles shown a dark brown yellow color, but under UV light (365 nm) a whitish blue emission was observed in all cases.

The Fig. 1 shown fluorescence spectra of CDs and M@CDs at several excitation wavelengths (330, 350, 370, 390, 410, 430, 450, 470, and 490 nm), and emission was observed in the range of 440–610 nm. Fig. 1a shows the typical fluorescence spectra obtained from the CDs, revealing that they are highly fluorescent. The Metal nanoparticles showed no fluorescence, whereas the fluorescence of the M@CD nanoparticles was slightly lower in intensity than the pristine CDs. The reduction in fluorescence intensity is an indirect evidence for the incorporation of metal in the CDs. The same result was also observed for the Sn@CDs (Fig. 1d), whereas Zn@CDs (Fig. 1c) had a narrower fluorescence spectrum, albeit with similar intensity as Sn@CDs. The Ag (Fig. 1e) and Au (Fig. 1f) doped CDs exhibited less intense fluorescence. The reduced PL intensity of M@CDs can serve as an evidence for the successful doping of the metal in CDs. Furthermore, the doping of the metal was confirmed by UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The formation of the M@CDs was demonstrated also by the UV/Vis absorption spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 . The CDs and M@CDs showed two clear absorption peaks, at approximately at 286 and 355 nm, which indicate the presence of CDs [19]. Furthermore, M@CDs showed nearly similar absorption peaks, which thus indicate the doping of the CDs by a metal. Moreover, in the case of Ag@CDs and Au@CDs, additional small absorbance peaks are evident at 430 and 520 nm, respectively; their presence, at the plasmon resonances of these two precious metals, provides further evidence for the doping of silver and gold in CDs.

The chemical composition of the pristine CDs and M@CDs was analyzed by XPS to prove the doping. The XPS spectra of the CDs (see Fig. S1a in the supporting information) contained two major bands at around 288.66 and 532.61 eV, which correspond to C 1s and O 1s, respectively.For the Sn@CDs, characteristic peaks corresponding to C 1s (284.95 eV), O 1s (532.62 eV) and Sn 3d (487.50 eV) were observed in the XPS spectrum (Fig. S1b), confirming the expected composition of C, O and zero-valent tin. Similarly, for the Ga@CDs, characteristic peaks corresponding to C 1s (284.92 eV), O 1s (532.345 eV), Ga 2p (1118.621 eV), Ga 3p (106.394 and 109.473 eV) and Ga 3d (20.290 eV) were observed in the XPS spectrum (Fig. S1c), confirming the composition of C, O, and Ga. The XPS analyses revealed that the doping Sn and Ga in CDs, which originated from the corresponding molten metals.The synthesis mechanism of M@CDs, from PEG-400 and molten metal, is likely to involve several steps, such as a fragmentation process, formation of hydronium ion, polymerization, aromatization, and carbonization.

The QYs of the CDs and M@CDs fluorescence were determined by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 355 nm) and the absorbance values at the same wavelength with that of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54%). Specifically, the absorbance values of prepared samples (CDs and M@CDs) and quinine sulfate were measured at 355 nm, as described in our previous reports [54]. The values of the quantum yield were calculated according to the following equation:
QY = Q
Y
st
×
A x
A
st
×
P
L
st
P
L x
×
n x
n
st
2
× 100 %

Where the subscripts st and x denote quinine sulfate standard and prepared samples respectively, A is the absorbance, PL is the area of the emission peak, and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The QY was measured for the CDs and M@CDs. The CDs emitted a strong blue-green luminescence, and the QY was estimated to be ∼16% using quinine sulfate as reference. Table 1 presents the QYs of the prepared all samples. The fluorescence behavior of the CDs is influenced by their surface functional groups, surface defect sites, and metal doping located on the surface, which are responsible for trapping of excited-state energy leading to the highest quantum yield of M@CDs. Furthermore, the measurement of fluorescence intensity as function of time confirmed that the CDs and M@CDs were photo stable, as no significant photo bleaching behavior was observed upon continuous excitation (λex = 365 nm) for 24 h.

ICP-OES examination was used to determine the metal doping in the M@CDs nanoparticles. The samples were prepared for analysis in the following manner: 1 mL of M@CD suspension in PEG-400 was dissolved in the mixture of 0.5 M HNO3 and 0.5 M HCl in a 100 mL beaker, which was heated on a hot plate to 70 °C for 4 h. Then, the HNO3 was vaporized, and the residue was diluted with double distilled water. This procedure was repeated thrice to reduce the concentration of acidic HNO3. The final solutions were analyzed for metal (Ag, Au, Ga, Sn and Zn) by ICP-OES, and the results (Table 1) show different doping levels depending on the nature of the metal. We found a gradual increase in metal content in the M@CDs with increasing metal concentration in the synthesis solution. However, this content approached a limit when the amount of metal in the solution was greater than 250 mg. In addition, Table 1 presents the quantum yield (QY), size based on TEM analysis, stability, and toxicity of each carbon dot.

The morphology of the M@CDs (Fig. 4 ) was analyzed by TEM, and the particle size was found to be in the range of 4–9 nm. The shapes of the pristine CD nanoparticles were spherical with disc shape (Fig. 4a, b) and particle size of 3–7 nm. The elemental composition of the CDs was determined by EDS and XPS; only C and O were found, as expected. The effect of metal doping was studied by comparing with the pristine CDs. As shown in Fig. 4, the pristine CDs and M@CDs have similar morphology and lattice parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interactions of metal-doped carbon dots with living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the cytotoxicity of the prepared carbon dots was investigated by XTT cell viability assay. We studied the effect of the CDs and M@CDs on the viability of PC12 neural cells. The corresponding results are summarized in Fig. 5 . Cells were treated with prepared samples of CDs at concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 0.5 mg/mL. Cell viability was measured at two different time points: day 1 and day 3 following the treatment of nanoparticles. The results were normalized to the control, i.e. cells without any CDs. When treated with CDs, PC12 cells remained viable after day 1. No significant difference in viability was observed for the concentration range of 0.01–0.2 mg/mL. However, at the highest concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, a significant decrease in cell viability was observed. Similarly, M@CDs showed good cell viability to PC12 cells for 72 h of incubation. All prepared samples were showed more than 100% of cell viability even at concentration of 0.2 mg/mL except Zn@CDs (88%). This is may be due to the doping of metal and doping level. From Table 1, the order of metal doping level (in ppm) are in order of Au (440) = Ag (440) > Sn (410) > Ga (340) > Zn (230). Hence, due to lower doping of Zn, Zn@CDs showed higher cytotoxicity. The cytotoxicity at higher concentrations is widely reported [55,56]. The cytotoxicity of CDs was widely studied, and due to its low toxicity, they are considered as promising fluorescence probe in biomedical applications [57]. The cytotoxicity of CDs also depends on many other parameters, such as particle size, stability, surface coating and functionalization, and aggregation [58]. The prepared samples of metal doped CDs were found to be non-toxic for PC12 cells even at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL, which enables us to use them in neural tissue engineering applications.

The interactions of the prepared samples (CDs and M@CDs) with neurons were performed using PC12 cells differentiation. The cells treated with the prepared samples showed no effect on the ability to differentiate. As compared to the control, the nanoparticles had no effect on the differentiation of the PC12 cells, the neurite outgrowth, or the network formation. Similar results were observed with different nanoparticles as we reported in our previous publications [48,59]. The progress in the differentiation of the cells was studied by imaging at different time intervals – after 12 h and 1, 2 and 5 days. The corresponding representative images are presented in Fig. 6 , which clearly indicates that there is no significant difference in differentiation in the presence of metal-doped CD nanoparticles. It was thus concluded that the prepared samples (CDs and M@CDs) are not toxic and had no effect on the differentiation process of the PC12 cells. Hence, M@CDs and CDs can be promising materials in neural tissue engineering applications.

PC12 cells were incubated for 24 h with nanoparticles with a wide range of concentrations – 0.01, 0.05 and 0.1 mg/mL. To study the uptake of nanoparticles by the cells the fluorescence intensity was measured by flow cytometry. Fig. 7 shows the uptake of nanoparticles by the cells, which was calculated on the basis of Fig. S2. An increase in fluorescence was observed with increasing nanoparticle concentrations for all samples. This further supports the use of the fluorescence of CDs in PC12 cells.

Due to the fluorescence property of the prepared samples, they can be detected within the cells by fluorescent confocal microscopy. To confirm internalization of nanoparticles into the cells, confocal imaging was done at different Z-stack levels and images were examined at an intracellular focal plane. We investigated the internalization of the prepared CDs into cells. Corresponding confocal images are presented in Fig. 8 , clearly demonstrating the internalization of all CDs. Fluorescent signals are observed in cells incubated with CDs, as well as in cells incubated with M@CDs. On the other hand, no fluorescent signal was detected in cells incubated without any nanoparticles. Highest levels of labeling could be seen after incubation with the Zn-CDs. These promising results open new applications of CDs and M@CDs in biomedical studies, targeted drug delivery and bio-imaging.

In summary, we have successfully prepared carbon dots with doping of five different metals (Ag, Au, Ga, Sn and Zn) through sonochemical process. The synthesized CDs with and without doping were highly dispersible, stable and uniformly sized. The physical and chemical characterization of CDs, Ga@CDs, Sn@CDs, Ag@CDs, Zn@CDs and Au@CDs confirmed the formation of particles with control nanoscale sizes. The prepared M@CDs showed high fluorescence and very good biocompatibility. These fluorescent metal-doped carbon dots were also shown to be efficient for interactions with neuron-like cells, optimal for cell labeling with no negative effect on neuronal differentiation and growth. The present approach offers new insight into the production of cheap, highly sensitive and selective materials for various biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA) and Bar-Ilan University for providing infrastructure. Dr. Raj Kumar is thankful to the Planning and Budgeting Committee of the Council of Higher Education for awarding postdoctoral fellowship grateful for a PBC-Israel postdoctoral fellowship and thanks Michal Marcus, Neta Zilony, Sharon Cohen and Hadas Schori for assistance and Avi Jacob for help with confocal imaging. This work was supported by the Israel Science Foundation Individual grant #1053/15 (O.S.).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2018.11.017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
